title: Mass Casualty Tag And Distribution
description: An acute-care operations workflow that coordinates urgent intake, prioritization, and constrained resource assignment under clinical safety rules. Focus case: mass casualty tag and distribution.
assumptions: Resources (spaces, equipment, teams, tags, stretchers) are modeled with explicit availability flags and must be reserved before being considered allocated.
Eligibility relations (which patient can use which resource) are static constraints computed before runtime and do not themselves change availability.
Facility acceptance for transfer is an explicit two-step interaction: request and confirm; confirmed acceptance marks a transfer relation used by downstream actions.
A finalized disposition flag prevents further registrations or allocations for that casualty.
risks: Race conditions during concurrent reservation operations could produce conflicting allocations if external synchronization is not enforced.
Misinterpretation of 'eligibility' relations as reservations may lead to accidental double-booking; reservations must explicitly flip availability flags.
If a receiving facility acceptance action is modeled as a passive compatibility check rather than an active confirmation, it can misrepresent capacity and cause unsafe transfers.
Over-reliance on single clinician or single-space assignment assumptions may not generalize to surge scenarios where pooled staffing is used.
Incorrect clearing of intermediate flags (e.g., evaluated, ready_for_transfer) can block valid patient progression or prematurely finalize disposition.
